nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving forest streams, pools, and swampy areas with dense leaf litter and overhanging vegetation. Water is soft, acidic, and tannin-stained, shaded beneath a closed rainforest canopy.

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Java moss, Cryptocoryne spp.)
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce jumping
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to acidify and tint water
driftwood and twisted roots for structure
dark substrate (fine dark sand or leaf-litter bed)
tight-fitting lid — mandatory given high escape risk
gentle filtration (sponge filter) to avoid strong currents

diet.notes: The Striped Aphyosemion accepts a range of small live and frozen invertebrates, including fruit flies (Drosophila), micro worms, white worms, grindal worms, baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Most specimens will learn to accept high-quality small carnivore micro-pellets and freeze-dried foods over time, but offering variety with live and frozen items is strongly recommended for long-term health and breeding condition. Feed small portions twice daily and avoid overfeeding, which can quickly degrade water quality in the small tanks this species typically occupies.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras) in larger tanks
corydoras (small species — C. habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
other small non-competitive killifish in a species-only setup
avoidWith: larger or aggressive fish that may intimidate or harass
fast-moving boisterous species that outcompete at feeding
other Aphyosemion males without ample territory (male conspecific aggression risk)
shrimp smaller than 2 cm (fry and juveniles may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Alexander Prokoshev; 1680x1260. Sharp, vibrant male in full colour showing the diagnostic rows of red dotted stripes on a turquoise body, green eye-ring, and yellow/red lyretail extensions, with a female behind. Best representation of the species.
Alexander Prokoshev; 1024x768. Crisp foreground male with clear red striping and turquoise flank plus a softer female behind; good pair/behaviour shot.
Alexander Prokoshev; 1326x994. Whole male in a planted setup showing red striped pattern and green sheen; slightly soft focus so kept as a non-recommended extra.

summary: The Striped Aphyosemion is a non-annual killifish from Gabon’s rainforest streams, males displaying vivid red horizontal stripes on a turquoise-green body with lyretail fin extensions. Generally considered one of the more approachable killifish for newcomers to the group, it is well suited to small planted tanks. A tight-fitting lid is essential — this species is a reliable jumper.
careNotes: The Striped Aphyosemion (Aphyosemion striatum) is generally considered one of the more forgiving species in its genus and is often recommended as an entry point into killifish keeping, though it still demands soft, slightly acidic water and attentive husbandry. Multiple locality forms exist — for example, those labelled GA 1, GA 2, Libreville, and Lastourville — which differ subtly in male coloration patterns. Collectors often maintain these forms separately to preserve locality purity. A tight-fitting, gapless lid is non-negotiable: A. striatum tends to be a strong jumper and will exploit any opening, including gaps around equipment cables. A mature planted nano tank of 40–60 L with gentle sponge filtration, dim lighting softened by floating plants, and a leaf-litter bed closely replicates its tannin-stained rainforest stream habitat. Water should be kept soft, with a general hardness of 1–10 dGH and carbonate hardness of 1–5 dKH, and pH in the range of 6.0–7.0; peat filtration or Indian almond leaves can help condition harder tap water toward these values. Temperature should stay within 20–26°C — avoid overheating, which stresses the fish and can shorten lifespan. Weekly partial water changes of 20–25% are important to prevent nitrate accumulation in small tanks. Males can be aggressive toward one another in confined spaces; a single pair or a trio of one male and two females is the safest social arrangement in most aquaria. In larger, heavily planted tanks, two pairs may coexist without serious conflict. This species is a non-annual killifish and does not require diapause (the dormancy period that annual killifish use to survive dry seasons) — its eggs develop continuously in water.
breedingNotes: The Striped Aphyosemion is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants such as Java moss, in yarn spawning mops, or directly among leaf litter. A dedicated breeding tank of 20–30 L fitted with a spawning mop or a clump of Java moss is the most common approach. Water should be very soft and acidic, with a pH of 6.0–6.5 and general hardness of 1–5 dGH. Eggs hatch in approximately 14–21 days at 22–24°C. Unlike annual killifish, this species does not undergo diapause: eggs develop continuously in water and must not be dried out. Fry are free-swimming immediately on hatching and will accept infusoria, micro worms, and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from the first day. Eggs and young fry should be separated from adults promptly to prevent predation. A practical method is to swap spawning mops daily, rinse the eggs gently in clean tank water, and incubate them in a shallow dish or separate container until hatching. Conditioning broodstock on live foods before and during spawning is reported to accelerate breeding activity and improve egg yield.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyosemion striatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Gabon), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (~4 cm), habitat (shaded forest streams with tannin-stained water), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates), tank setup recommendations, breeding methodology, and locality form notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyosemion striatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (to 5.0 cm TL), temperature range (20–26°C), native range (Gabon), habitat description, and diet (invertivore). Confirmed non-annual status.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used as hobbyist trade perspective. Corroborated small tank size suitability (20–30+ gal not needed; 10 gal / ~40 L adequate for pairs), carnivore diet with live/frozen foods, escape risk (strong jumper, tight lid required), and intermediate care classification. Confirmed general non-annual group behaviour.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Killifish Species Guide
additional.notes: Provided context on Aphyosemion genus husbandry: soft acidic water, tannin staining, locality form keeping practices, non-annual egg-scattering breeding methodology, and recommended approach of species-only or quiet community setups. Confirmed 14–21 day egg incubation period at typical temperatures.

additional.name: American Killifish Association — Aphyosemion striatum species account
additional.notes: Specialist killifish society source. Provided locality form details (GA codes referencing Gabon collection sites), breeding mop technique, egg incubation in water vs. damp peat methodology, fry food recommendations (micro worms, BBS nauplii), and notes on male coloration variation between locality forms.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs in fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Eggs are non-diapause and develop continuously in water, hatching in approximately 14–21 days at 22–24°C. Adults should be separated from eggs promptly to prevent predation. Fry accept micro worms and brine shrimp nauplii from first feeding. Conditioning adults on live foods before spawning tends to improve egg yield.
